name: resnest

members.name: ResNeStConfig
members.signature: ResNeStConfig(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, layers: tuple[int, ...] = (3, 4, 6, 3), radix: int = 2, groups: int = 1, avg_down: bool = True, avd: bool = True, avd_first: bool = False, stem_width: int = 32, deep_stem: bool = True, dropout: float = 0.0, zero_init_residual: bool = False)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, layers: tuple[int, ...] = (3, 4, 6, 3), radix: int = 2, groups: int = 1, avg_down: bool = True, avd: bool = True, avd_first: bool = False, stem_width: int = 32, deep_stem: bool = True, dropout: float = 0.0, zero_init_residual: bool = False)

members.extended:
`layers`           — per-stage block repetition counts.
`radix`            — number of split branches in SplitAttn conv.
`groups`           — cardinality (number of convolution groups).
`avg_down`         — use AvgPool + 1×1 Conv for downsampling shortcuts.
`avd`              — use averaged downsampling (AvgPool) around SplitAttn.
`avd_first`        — place the AvgPool before (True) or after (False) SplitAttn.
`stem_width`       — channel width of each deep-stem conv (output = stem_width*2).
`deep_stem`        — use a 3-convolution deep stem instead of a single 7×7 conv.

members.name: ResNeSt
members.extended:
Implements the Split-Attention augmentation of the ResNet
topology from Zhang et al., "ResNeSt: Split-Attention
Networks", CVPR Workshops 2022 ([arXiv:2004.08955](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.08955)).  Each
bottleneck's $3 \times 3$ is replaced by a *Split-Attention*
block that fuses ResNeXt cardinality, SKNet kernel selection,
and SENet channel attention into a single unified primitive.
The block splits the feature map into $K$ cardinal
groups, then further splits each group into $R$ parallel
*radix* branches, and computes per-radix softmax attention
weights

$$
a_{r, c} = \frac{\exp(z_{r, c})}{\sum_{r'=1}^{R} \exp(z_{r', c})},
\qquad V_c = \sum_{r=1}^{R} a_{r, c} \cdot U_{r, c}.
$$

Two further refinements complete the architecture: a *deep
stem* (three 3×3 convolutions replacing the original 7×7
stem) and an *average-pool downsampling* path that replaces
strided convolutions in residual shortcuts and inside the
Split-Attention block itself.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use the factory functions
(`resnest_50`, `resnest_101`, …) for
paper-cited variants.

examples:
Build a ResNeSt-50 backbone and run a forward pass:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnest import resnest_50
>>> backbone = resnest_50()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = backbone(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(2, 2048, 7, 7)
notes:
When $R = 1$ the Split-Attention block degenerates to
ResNeXt; at $R \geq 2$ it generalises SKNet to arbitrary
radix counts with a softmax (rather than two-way sigmoid)
gate.  ResNeSt-50 outperforms plain ResNet-50 by roughly 3
ImageNet top-1 percentage points at the same parameter count,
and serves as a strong backbone for downstream detection /
segmentation pipelines.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: conv1
attributes.description:
Deep stem (three 3×3 convolutions) or single 7×7 conv,
depending on `config.deep_stem`.

attributes.name: bn1
attributes.description: BatchNorm paired with the stem output.

attributes.name: layer1, layer2, layer3, layer4
attributes.description:
The four Split-Attention residual stages.  Output strides
are 4, 8, 16, and 32 relative to the input.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Per-stage descriptor (channels + reduction factor) exposed
via `BackboneMixin`.

members.name: ResNeStForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: ResNeSt with global-average-pooled linear classification head.
members.extended:
Combines a `ResNeSt` backbone with global average
pooling, an optional `lucid.nn.Dropout` (probability
`config.dropout`), and a linear projection to
`config.num_classes` logits.  When `labels` are supplied
to `forward`, a cross-entropy loss is computed and
returned alongside the logits.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture spec.  Use the `*_cls` factory functions
(`resnest_50_cls`, `resnest_101_cls`, …) for
paper-cited configurations.

examples:
Run inference on a batch of 224×224 RGB images:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnest import resnest_50_cls
>>> model = resnest_50_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(4, 1000)
notes:
The classification flow is



$$
\text{logits} = W \,\operatorname{Drop}\!\left(
    \operatorname{GAP}(\,\mathrm{backbone}(x)\,)
\right) + b.
$$

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: conv1, bn1, act1, maxpool, layer1, layer2, layer3, layer4
attributes.description:
Same backbone components as on `ResNeSt`; see that
class for shape semantics.

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description:
Global average pool collapsing the final feature map to
`1 × 1`.

attributes.name: classifier
attributes.description:
Built by `ClassificationHeadMixin._build_classifier` —
a `lucid.nn.Linear` (or `lucid.nn.Sequential`
with dropout) projecting from 2048 channels to
`config.num_classes`.

members.name: resnest_14
members.extended:
Builds a `ResNeSt` with block repeats `[1, 1, 1, 1]`
over four stages — a lightweight variant of the ResNeSt family
targeted at low-latency deployments.  Uses `radix = 2` and
`stem_width = 32`.  Approximately 10.6M parameters.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored — the returned model is randomly initialised.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `ResNeStConfig`.

returns.description:
Backbone with the ResNeSt-14 configuration applied (or
with `overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnest import resnest_14
>>> model = resnest_14()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 2048, 7, 7)
notes:
See Zhang et al., "ResNeSt: Split-Attention Networks", CVPR
Workshops 2022 ([arXiv:2004.08955](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.08955)).  Final-stage output is 2048
channels.

members.name: resnest_14_cls
members.summary: ResNeSt-14 image classifier (backbone + GAP + linear head).
members.extended:
Builds a `ResNeStForImageClassification` with the
ResNeSt-14 backbone (block repeats `[1, 1, 1, 1]`) followed
by global average pooling and a linear projection to
`config.num_classes` (default 1000 for ImageNet-1k).
Approximately 10.6M parameters — the lightweight variant of
the ResNeSt family.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `ResNeStConfig`.

returns.description:
Classifier with the ResNeSt-14 configuration applied (or
with `overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnest import resnest_14_cls
>>> model = resnest_14_cls(num_classes=10)
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 10)
notes:
See Zhang et al., "ResNeSt: Split-Attention Networks", CVPR
Workshops 2022 ([arXiv:2004.08955](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.08955)).

members.name: resnest_26
members.extended:
Builds a `ResNeSt` with block repeats `[2, 2, 2, 2]`
over four stages — a middle-ground variant between the
lightweight ResNeSt-14 and the canonical ResNeSt-50.  Uses
`radix = 2` and `stem_width = 32`.  Approximately 17.1M
parameters.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides

(1, 2048, 7, 7)

ResNeSt-26 backbone (block repeats `[2, 2, 2, 2]`) followed

members.name: resnest_50
members.extended:
Builds a `ResNeSt` with ResNet-50 topology
(`[3, 4, 6, 3]`), `radix = 2`, and the canonical
`stem_width = 32` deep stem.  Approximately 27.5M parameters
and 81.1% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy in Zhang et al., 2022
(Table 4) — roughly 5 points higher than plain ResNet-50 at
the same parameter budget.  The default ResNeSt configuration.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `ResNeStConfig`
(e.g. `radix=4` to widen the split-attention).

returns.description:
Backbone with the ResNeSt-50 configuration applied (or
with `overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnest import resnest_50
>>> model = resnest_50()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 2048, 7, 7)
notes:
See Zhang et al., "ResNeSt: Split-Attention Networks", CVPR
Workshops 2022 ([arXiv:2004.08955](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.08955)), Table 4.

members.name: resnest_50_cls
members.signature: resnest_50_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: ResNeSt50Weights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: ResNeSt-50 image classifier (backbone + GAP + linear head).
members.extended:
Builds a `ResNeStForImageClassification` with the
canonical ResNeSt-50 backbone (`[3, 4, 6, 3]`, `radix = 2`)
followed by global average pooling and a linear projection.
Approximately 27.5M parameters and 81.1% ImageNet-1k top-1
accuracy in Zhang et al., 2022 (Table 4) — the canonical
ResNeSt configuration.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`ResNeSt50Weights.IN1K`); a tag
string → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive with
`weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `ResNeStConfig`
(typically `num_classes` to retarget the classifier).

returns.description:
Classifier with the ResNeSt-50 configuration applied (or
with `overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnest import resnest_50_cls
>>> model = resnest_50_cls(num_classes=10)
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 10)
notes:
See Zhang et al., "ResNeSt: Split-Attention Networks", CVPR
Workshops 2022 ([arXiv:2004.08955](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.08955)), Table 4.  Pretrained weights are
converted from timm's `resnest50d.in1k` and hosted under
`lucid-dl/resnest-50`.

members.name: resnest_101
members.extended:
Builds a `ResNeSt` with ResNet-101 topology
(`[3, 4, 23, 3]`), `radix = 2`, and a wider
`stem_width = 64` deep stem.  Approximately 48.3M parameters
and 82.8% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy in Zhang et al., 2022
(Table 4).  Higher-accuracy drop-in replacement for ResNeSt-50.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `ResNeStConfig`.

returns.description:
Backbone with the ResNeSt-101 configuration applied (or
with `overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnest import resnest_101
>>> model = resnest_101()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 2048, 7, 7)
notes:
See Zhang et al., "ResNeSt: Split-Attention Networks", CVPR
Workshops 2022 ([arXiv:2004.08955](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.08955)), Table 4.

members.name: resnest_269
members.extended:
Builds a `ResNeSt` with the very deep topology
`[3, 30, 48, 8]`, `radix = 2`, and `stem_width = 64` —
the deepest variant from Zhang et al., 2022.  Approximately
110.9M parameters and 84.5% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy
(Table 4) at 416×416 input resolution.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `ResNeStConfig`.

returns.description:
Backbone with the ResNeSt-269 configuration applied (or
with `overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnest import resnest_269
>>> model = resnest_269()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 2048, 7, 7)
notes:
See Zhang et al., "ResNeSt: Split-Attention Networks", CVPR
Workshops 2022 ([arXiv:2004.08955](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.08955)), Table 4.  Memory footprint
is substantial; consider `zero_init_residual=True` for
stable large-batch training.

members.name: resnest_269_cls

Builds a `ResNeStForImageClassification` with the
deepest paper-cited backbone (`[3, 30, 48, 8]`,
`stem_width = 64`) followed by global average pooling and a
linear classifier.  Approximately 110.9M parameters and 84.5%
ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy in Zhang et al., 2022 (Table 4) at
416×416 input resolution.

>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
See Zhang et al., "ResNeSt: Split-Attention Networks", CVPR

is substantial.  Pretrained weights are converted from timm's

members.name: ResNeSt50Weights
members.extended:
Single ImageNet-1k checkpoint converted from timm's
`resnest50d.in1k` (Zhang et al., 2022; ~27.5M params, 81.1%
top-1).  Evaluated at 224 crop / 256 resize / bilinear.

members.name: ResNeSt101Weights
members.extended:
Single ImageNet-1k checkpoint converted from timm's
`resnest101e.in1k` (Zhang et al., 2022; ~48.3M params, 82.8%
top-1).  Evaluated at 256 crop / 293 resize / bilinear.

members.name: ResNeSt200Weights
members.extended:
Single ImageNet-1k checkpoint converted from timm's
`resnest200e.in1k` (Zhang et al., 2022; ~70.2M params, 83.9%
top-1).  Evaluated at 320 crop / 352 resize / bicubic.

members.name: ResNeSt269Weights
members.extended:
Single ImageNet-1k checkpoint converted from timm's
`resnest269e.in1k` (Zhang et al., 2022; ~110.9M params, 84.5%
top-1) — the deepest paper-cited variant.  Evaluated at 416 crop /
448 resize / bicubic.

citation: Zhang, Hang, et al. "ResNeSt: Split-Attention Networks." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops, 2022, pp. 2736–2746.
theory:
ResNeSt fuses three previously separate ideas — ResNeXt's
**cardinality**, SKNet's **soft kernel selection**, and SENet's
**channel-wise attention** — into a single unified
*Split-Attention* block that can replace the central
$3\times3$ convolution of any ResNet bottleneck.

The block first splits the input into $K$ groups
(cardinality), and inside each group it further splits the
feature map into $R$ parallel branches called *radixes*.
Each radix runs a different transformation
$\mathcal{F}_r$ and produces a $C$ -channel feature
map $U_r$.  The branch outputs are summed to form a
single combined response $U = \sum_r U_r$, which is then
pooled and passed through a small bottleneck network to emit
*per-radix, per-channel* attention weights:



$$
a_{r, c} = \frac{ \exp\big( z_{r, c} \big) }
                { \sum_{r' = 1}^{R} \exp\big( z_{r', c} \big) },
\qquad \sum_{r=1}^{R} a_{r, c} = 1 \;\; \forall c.
$$

The block's output is the radix-weighted sum



$$
V_c = \sum_{r=1}^{R} a_{r, c} \cdot U_{r, c},
$$

applied independently inside every cardinal group and then
concatenated.  When $R = 1$ the block reduces to ResNeXt;
when $R \geq 2$ it generalises SKNet to arbitrary radix
counts with a softmax (rather than two-way sigmoid) gate.

Two further refinements complete the architecture.  A
**deep stem** replaces the original $7\times7$ ResNet
stem with three $3\times3$ convolutions, and an
**average-pool downsampling** path (controlled by the
`avg_down` and `avd` fields) replaces strided
convolutions in residual shortcuts and inside the Split-Attention
block itself.  With these changes ResNeSt-50 outperforms a
plain ResNet-50 by roughly 3 ImageNet top-1 percentage points
at the same parameter count, and serves as a strong backbone
for downstream detection / segmentation tasks.
